title: Amphisphaeria guizhouensis Q. Yang, Q. R. Li & Cheewangkoon 2025, sp. nov.
author: Yang, Qing
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description: Amphisphaeria guizhouensis  Q. Yang, Q.R. Li & Cheewangkoon,  sp. nov.  (Fig. 2).  MycoBank number: MB859927   Etymology:  —The specific epithet “  guizhouensis  ” refers to the type location, Guizhou Province, China.   Description:  Saprobic on dead branches of an unidentified woody plant.  Sexual morph:  Ascomata   265–341 × 405–547 µm ( x  = 295 × 484 µm, n = 5), black, scattered, with a cracked surface, immersed beneath the host epidermis, spherical to subspherical;  Ostiole  papillate, 129–164 µm wide ( x  =142 µm, n = 5), yellowish-brown centric.  Peridium  26–42 µm thick, composed of layers of cortical cells, the inner layer is reddish-brown and triangular, while the outer layer consists of brownish-black to black cells of  textura angularis.  Paraphyses  3.7–5.5 µm wide ( x  = 4.4 µm, n = 20), hyaline, cellular, septate with constrictions, guttulate.  Asci  142–168 × 7–9.8 µm ( x  = 156 × 8 µm, n = 30), 8-spored, unitunicate, cylindrical, with a short cylindrical pedicel and a distinct apical ring, J- in Melzer’s reagent.  Ascospores  19–23.8 × 4.9–6.3 µm ( x  = 20.9 × 5.7 µm, n = 30), L/W ratio 3.6, uniseriate, ellipsoidal to fusiform, guttulate, hyaline when immature, becoming pale brown at maturity, 3-septate, with round ends.  Asexual morph : Undetermined.    Culture characteristics:  Colony on PDA is irregular, reaching 40 mm in diameter after 14 days at 28°C in a constant-temperature incubator. The surface is smooth, with dense, fluffy, compact, and dry mycelia. In the lateral view, the colony is raised with a well-defined edge. From above, the colony is white to cream-colored. From below, the edge is pale yellow, the center is orange-yellow, and the core is brown. Yellow pigments are produced during cultivation.   Specimen examined:  CHINA, Guizhou Province, Tongren City, Foding Mountain Forest Nature Reserve, 27°17′33.20″N, 108°0′36.02″E, elevation 1043 m, on dead branches of an unidentified woody plant, 7 August 2024, Qing Yang, 2024FDSYJZ77 (GMB5901 Holotype), ex-type living culture GMBC5901;  ibid ., (KUN-HKAS 146995, Isotype).   Additional material examined (paratype):  CHINA, Guizhou Province, Tongren City, Foding Mountain Forest Nature Reserve, 27°19′37.30″N, 108°8′40.62″E, elevation 650 m, on dead branch of unidentified woody plant, 8 August 2024, Qing Yang, 2024FDSDCY72 (paratype GMB5902, exparatype GMBC5902).   Notes:   Amphisphaeria guizhouensis  is placed within the genus  Amphisphaeria  based on the present phylogenetic analyses and morphology (Figure 1). Multi-loci phylogeny shows that  A. guizhouensis  forms a sister clade with  A. curvaticonidia  with 98% ML and 0.99 BYPP support value (Figure 1). In BLAST searches, the ITS and LSU sequence of  A. guizhouensis  shows 93.6% and 95.55% similarity to  A. curvaticonidia . Moreover, the new species can be distinguished by its larger ascomata (265–341 × 405–547 µm vs. 320–390 × 360–410 µm), narrower asci (142–168 × 7–9.8 µm vs. 121–162 × 10.5–17.5 µm), and narrower ascospore (19–23.8 × 4.9–6.3 µm vs. 17–23 × 6–9 µm). The apical ring of asci in  A. guizhouensis  did not show a blue colour change with Melzer’s reagent, but  A. curvaticonidia  showed the color change to blue (Samarakoon  et al . 2020). The ITS and LSU sequences of  Amphisphaeria guizhouensis  showed 93.68% and 96.26% similarity to  Lepteutypa qujingensis  (HMAS 290478). Morphologically,  A. guizhouensis  can be distinguished from  L. qujingensis  by its thicker peridium (26–42 vs. 10–25 µm), narrower paraphyses (3.7–5.5 vs. 6.5–12 µm), and larger asci (142–168 × 7–9.8 µm vs. 100–140 × 4.5–8.5 µm). In addition,  A. guizhouensis  is J- in Melzer’s reagent, whereas  L. qujingensis  is J+ (Dissanayake  et al . 2020). The new species also differs from  A. parvispora  by having larger asci (142–168 × 7–9.8 µm vs. 62–105 × 5–6.5 μm) and ascospores (19–23.8 × 4.9–6.3 µm vs. 9.5–11.5 × 3–4 μm) (Samarakoon  et al . 2022). Moreover, the asci of  A. guizhouensis  are larger than those of  A. ailaoshanensis  (142–168 × 7–9.8 µm vs. 70–100 × 7–10 μm) (Dissanayake  et al . 2024).
